coredata.dc:title: Zener diode behavior of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Nag, Jadupati
Rawat, Kamla
Asokan, K.
Kanjilal, D.
Bohidar, H.B.
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized, using ammonia as nitrogen and d-glucose as carbon source, using a facile microwave-assisted protocol, where the N/C ratio could be varied from 0.19 to 0.25 (% w/w, determined from EDAX). The as-synthesized quantum dots consisting of one to three graphene monolayers exhibited high crystalline morphology with an average size of 1.8 ± 0.2 nm. HRTEM data showed the presence of both pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N structures. Semiconductor profile of the N-GQDs was extensively probed, and it was noticed that the optical bandgap, knee-voltage and break-down voltage varied linearly with the N/C ratio. The doped samples showed with an optical bandgap ≈5.3 eV at the maximum nitrogen doping yielding n-type semiconductor property. Clear Zener diode attributes with a large forward bias current (100–200 mA), and a smaller reverse bias current of typically half that value was found.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene
Microwave
Functional nanomaterials
Optical properties
Electrical properties

KR acknowledges receipt of DST-Inspire Faculty award from Department of Science and Technology , Government of India. We are thankful to the Advanced Research Instrumentation Facility (AIRF) of the University for allowing us access to the TEM, SEM/EDX, FTIR and Raman facility. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-GQDs on a Cu-grid coated with an ultrathin carbon film. (b–d) HRTEM images with three lattice fringes 0.25, 0.21, 0.40 nm, respectively. (c, d) show the existence of hexagonal and pentagonal crystal structure with lattice parameter of 0.21 and 0.4 nm. (e) Size distribution histogram. (f) crystallite size with N/C ratio clearly showing a linear dependence. Solid line shows least-squares fitting of the data to a straight line.
(a) Forward and (b) reverse bias I-V characteristics of N-GQDsmeasured at room temperature.
Temperature dependent forward and reverse bias I-V characteristics of N-doped GQDs.

(a) Dependence of knee and breakdown voltage with N/C ratio. See the remarkable linear dependence. Solid line is least-squares fitting of data to a straight line function.(b) linear dependence of bandgap on N/C ratio. Solid lines are least-squares fitting of the data to eqn. (3).

Elemental composition and bandgap, knee voltage, breakdown voltage, and Zener current shown for various samples. The figures under the glucose and ammonia solution refer to the amount of glucose dissolved in 5 ml of water, and the amount of ammonia solution added to it.

head.ce:title: Zener diode behavior of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Center for Nanosciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inter University Accelerator Centre, New Delhi, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physical Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India.
Corresponding author. Special Center for Nanosciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized, using ammonia as nitrogen and d-glucose as carbon source, using a facile microwave-assisted protocol, where the N/C ratio could be varied from 0.19 to 0.25 (% w/w, determined from EDAX). The as-synthesized quantum dots consisting of one to three graphene monolayers exhibited high crystalline morphology with an average size of 1.8 ± 0.2 nm. HRTEM data showed the presence of both pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N structures. Semiconductor profile of the N-GQDs was extensively probed, and it was noticed that the optical bandgap, knee-voltage and break-down voltage varied linearly with the N/C ratio. The doped samples showed with an optical bandgap ≈5.3 eV at the maximum nitrogen doping yielding n-type semiconductor property. Clear Zener diode attributes with a large forward bias current (100–200 mA), and a smaller reverse bias current of typically half that value was found.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and its derivatives have attracted much attention from both the experimental and theoretical scientific communities because these are considered promising materials for implementation in the next generation electronic devices [1].Semiconductor devices based on mechanisms alternative to classical charge transport become accessible owing to the unique properties of graphene that may one day help in the design of graphene based high speed transistors. Unfortunately, the direct implementation of the aforesaid objective is not possible in the case of pristine graphene which is a zero bandgap semiconductor. Graphene, with a two-dimensional array of sp2-bonded carbon atoms, exhibits high surface area (2620 m2 g−1), superior electrical and thermal conductivity, excellent mechanical strength and elasticity, and ease of modification [2]. Due to these unique physical and chemical properties, graphene provides an ideal base for electronics, sensors, composites, batteries, super-capacitors and hydrogen storage materials [3–7].

Mukherjee and Kaloni [8] performed an extensive band structure and density of states calculations to evaluate the effect of doping of graphene either by boron, nitrogen, or co-doped by B and N. Results indicated that upon doping by N (electron doping), the Dirac point in the graphene band structure shifted below the Fermi level and an energy gap appeared at the high symmetric K-point whereas in the case of B (hole doping), the Dirac point shifted above the Fermi level causing a bandgap to appear. Interestingly, upon co-doping by B and N, the energy gap between valence and conduction bands appeared at the Fermi level and the system behaved like a narrow bandgap semiconductor [8–10].

Several approaches have been successfully made in the past to dope nitrogen into graphene sheets, such as Joule annealing in NH3 [11], nitrogen plasma [12], chemical vapor deposition [13], arc-discharge of carbon electrode [14], hydrothermal reduction in the presence of hydrazine and ammonia [15], and surprisingly by hydrazine sonication [16]. However, convenient, cost-effective, up scalable and single step doping of graphene under ambient conditions is the need of the hour.

In this work, we focus on the implementation of nitrogen for the n-type doping of graphene. Since, nitrogen atom contains one additional electron and when it replaces a carbon atom in the graphene lattice, evolution of novel electronic properties can be envisaged which in our case exhibited remarkable current-voltage profile characteristic of Zener diode not reported hither to.

Analytical grade ammonia solution (30% (w/v)), quinine sulfate (purity ≥ 90.0%), and d-glucose (purity ≥ 99.5%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). The synthesis of N-doped graphene used a domestic microwave oven (Samsung, MC21044CP). All procedures were performed at room temperature 25 °C.

First, 10 g glucose was dissolved in 100 ml deionized (DI) water in a 250 ml conical flask to which 5 ml 30% ammonia solution was added [9,10]. Then, the reaction flask (without lid) was placed inside a domestic microwave oven and it was subjected to a radiation of 800 W for 1.5 min. Glucose solution was reduced using liquid ammonia at pH of ≈9.5 ± 0.5 under microwave conditions that resulted in the production of N-doped garphene (N-GQDs). In the reaction process glucose was used as the carbon source and ammonia acted as a catalyst assisting in dehydration of glucose molecule and as Nitrogen source. The use of a microwave method for synthesis assists in rapid production of N-GQDs as it accelerates the reaction process. The change in color of solution from transparent to pale yellow indicated the formation of N-GQDs. In this process, simultaneous reduction of glucose along with doping of nitrogen occurred. Therefore, we varied the concentration of nitrogen as well as glucose to understand the effect of precursors on the extent of doping. Subsequently, the reacted solution was stirred vigorously at 40 °C for 30 min to remove any residual ammonia. Finally, the solution was dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis membrane with molecular weight cutoff of 1 kDa and the aliquots received was used for further characterization. Table 1 designates the ID of samples made with different precursor composition. All procedures were performed at room temperature 25 °C unless stated otherwise.

UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a (CE 7200, Cecil Instruments, UK) spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette of 10 mm path length. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse, USA). Fluorescence and quantum yield (QY0 = 54% for standard quinine sulphate in dilute sulphuric acid) measurements were performed using the fluorescence spectrophotometer. For further details on these instruments can be obtained from Ref. [17]. The electrical measurements at room temperature and varying temperature (170 K–300 K) were performed using a Keithley 6517-B Electrometer connectedto a Lakeshore 325 temperature controller, following the two-probe method, interfaced by lab-view program.

A JEOL 2100F transmission electron microscope equipped with a tungsten filament and operated under accelerating voltage of 200 kV with maximum magnification = 150, 000× was used. XRD spectra were acquired using a Rigaku D/Max 2200 powder X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα target (λ = 1.54056 Å) and a Peltier-cooled solid-state detector. Raman spectra were recorded on a WITec alpha300 RA instrument using an excitation wavelength 532 nm obtained from a diode-pumped solid state laser.

The morphological studies and size distribution analysis of the samples were performed by transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 1 (a)) which showed that these had an average size of 1.8 ± 0.2 nm with spherical morphology and a Gaussian particle size distribution. Fig. 1(e) depicts the size histogram with typical values ranging between 1.1 and 2.6 nm. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show highly crystalline nature of these quantum dots with clear hexagonal and pentagonal lattice structures (Fig. 1(c and d))consistent with the well-known pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N crystal morphology. The estimated distance between the lattice fringes were d = 0.25, 0.21 and 0.4 nm (Fig. 1(b–d), which matched well with the in-plane lattice constant (d = 0.246 nm), in-plane lattice spacing (d100 = 0.213 nm), and the basal plane spacing (d002 = 0.33 nm) of graphite, respectively [18]. The basal plane spacing (0.4 nm) observed was larger than that of bulk graphite (0.33 nm) which may be due to the presence of nitrogen doping which enlarged the basal plane spacing [19]. In addition, samples revealed presence of some pyrrolic-N structures in HRTEM pictures. The fringe spacing here was 0.25 nm. Thus, we conclude that the as-synthesized material had both the pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N structures though the former was found in abundance.

Normally, Pyridinic-N bonds with two C atoms at the edges, and contribute a single p electron to the π system while Pyrrolic-N refers to N atoms that contribute two p electrons to the π system [20,21]. Quaternary-N is the N atoms that substitute for C atoms in a hexagonal ring. In both pyridinic and quaternary structures the hexagonal graphene lattice is retained (Fig. 1 (c)) while in pyrrolic structure morphology changes to a pentagon (Fig. 1 (d)). In these nitrogen substitutions, pyridinic-N and quaternary-N are sp2 and pyrrolic-N is sp3 hybridized. A single CC bond has energy of 346 kJ/mol and a bond length of 0.154 nm against the NC bond energy of 305 kJ/mol and bond length of 0.147 nm. Thus, the substitution of N at the C site will account for an energy gain of ≈40 kJ/mol and shrinking of the side by 0.007 nm. The N-substituted graphene hexagon has an area of 21 nm2 (=
3
3
2
S 2
; S = hexagon
side
length = 2.83
nm
). For pristine graphene it is 21 nm2. For pyrrolic-N, it changes to either 30 (bigger) or 14 (smaller) nm2(
=
5
2
S a ;
S = p e n t a g o n s i d e l e n g t h = 4
o r
2.8
nm ,
a n d a = a p o t h e m l e n g t h = 3
o r
2
nm ) .
Therefore, between the smaller hexagon and the typical pentagon unit there is a loss in the surface area by ≈ 7 nm2during hetero-atom doping(from HRTEM data analysis).

We could distinguish between different structures from the HR-TEM data. It is not possible to comment on the molecular size from this data. Shapes and sizes were obtained from the lattice fringe patterns, which show hexagonal or pentagonal structures, and the calculated d spacing is consistent with the reported data [18,19], and with the XRD data. However, we agree that such structures assignment at this stage is highly speculative.

Table 1 lists the elemental composition (from EDAX) and N/C ratio of the synthesized N-GQDs along with the signature of semiconductor properties. We observed an increase in the carbon content upon microwave reduction with simultaneous decrease in the oxygen content of the samples. This suggests the removal of oxygen moieties upon reduction.

The XRD profiles of the sample are shown in Fig. 2 that indicates a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 21ocorresponding to a d spacing of 0.402 nm along (002) direction, which is larger than the reported graphite d spacing (0.334 nm) [20]. This larger d 002 spacing is due to presence of highly disordered carbon and nitrogen atoms and can be attributed to the presence of functional groups and nitrogen doping which enlarges the basal plane spacing [22,23].The presence of additional less prominent peaks at 26.5°, and at 44.5° corresponds to the (002) and (101) graphitic planes respectively, indicating the very poor degree of graphitization. Few other additional peaks at 28° and 31° were observed due to the presence of residual glucose components present in the samples.

The estimated average crystallite size increased with the dopant concentration. For lowest doping average crystallite size calculated was 3.2 ± 0.1 nm, and in the case of highest doping it increased to 7.2 ± 0.2 nm, attributed to the replacement of C by N-atoms in the graphene lattice. The crystallite size determined by using eq. (1) is plotted in Fig. 3 to reveal a linear dependence given by
D =
D 0
+
m D
(
N C
)
. The slope m
D and the intercept D
0 were found to be 73 (% w/w)−1 and 3.82 nm, respectively.

The D, G, and 2D bands are the predominant features in the Raman spectrum of nitrogen doped graphene which are represented by peaks located at around 1320–1350, 1570–1585, and 2640−2680 cm−1 [24]. Additional peaks at 1602-1625 cm−1 have been reported too which are assigned to the intravalley, defect-induced, double-resonance process [25–28].First-order Raman scattering process yields the G band which corresponds to the doubly degenerate E2g phonons at the Brillouin zone. The D and 2D bands owe their origin to second order, double resonance process related to zone boundary phonons. Raman scattering theory stipulates that 2D mode involves two zone-boundary phonons while for the D mode involvement of one phonon and one defect is envisaged, and this band is required to be activated by defects unlike the 2D mode. Therefore, 2D mode is regularly noticed in all Raman spectra of graphene and nitrogen doped graphene samples even though the D band may be absent. It needs to be realized that nitrogen doping of graphene lattice causes incorporation of surface defects to the substrate lattice in most of the cases. The intensity ratio of 2D and the G band,
I
2 D
I G
has been used in the past to quantify the electron concentration in the N-graphene material [25].Linear increase in the intensity of the G band with doping concentration has also been reported [26].Cancado et al. used the formalism developed by Tunistra and Koenig to argue that the intensity ratio of the D and G bands ID/IG was inversely proportional to the in-plane crystallite size [27,28]. Zhang et al. observed that for the N-graphene samples crystallite size decreased remarkably with increase in nitrogen doping concentration [29–32].

Representative Raman spectra of our samples are depicted in Fig. 4 where identifiable peaks were found at 1345, 1602, and 2888 cm−1 for the doped samples. It has been noticed in the past that the G band moves to higher frequency in the case of nitrogen, or boron doping of graphene compared to the undoped sample [31–33] and in our case such behavior was observed. The band at 1345 and 1602 cm−1 are identified as the D and G bands while the peak at 2888 cm−1 is assigned to the 2D band. The ratio ID/IG apart from being a signature of the type of doping also defines the in-plane crystallite size in the Tunistra and Koenig model [27]. In our case, we find the typical in-plane crystallite size was ≈27 nm comparable to 21 nm reported by Zhang et al. [29] for pristine graphene it is about 65 nm [29]. It has been reported that ID/IG = 1.10 for graphene oxide, 1.13 for graphene and 1.15 for N-graphene. Our data revealed that it was 0.7 for our samples which is consistent with the Zhang et al. conclusion that at high nitrogen doping this ratio assume a value of 0.8. The 2ID/IG is an indicator of electron concentration in the material. Wang et al. obtained a value of 2ID/IG < 0.6 while in our case it was 0.3, they extend their calculations to evaluate the typical electron concentration for their material [23].

The UV–Visible absorption spectra of the synthesized N-GQDs are shown in Fig.S1 (Supplementary Information). We observed an increase in peak intensity along with red shifting of peaks with increasing amount of ammonia solution added to the reaction mixture implying higher dopant concentration. Furthermore, in S2, S4, S5 and S6 samples, two peaks were clearly observed at 270 and 310 nm that represent the characteristic π-π* electron transition of CN and CO, respectively [32,33].This suggests that the production of N-doped GQDs was favored when the concentration of nitrogen to carbon were in the ratio of 4:1. In the samples S1 and S3 this was not discernible, which could be due to poor resolution.

The fluorescence emission spectra of N -GQDs showed a redshift with increasing excitation wavelength. When the N-GQDs were excited at wavelengths in the range of λex = 250–360 nm, a shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength was observed from λem = 390–460 nm. Additionally, we observed an increase in peak intensity with wavelength. Figures S2 (Supplementary Information) show the fluorescence emission spectra of the samples prepared with varying concentration of ammonia and carbon. The excitation wavelength dependent shifting of emission spectra occurs because of variation in surface states assisted by oxygen (O π*) which results from the presence of COH, C = O, C O C) functional groups [32,33]. From the fluorescence emission spectra it was concluded that conversion of C π* to N π* and C π* to O π* causes redshift in the fluorescence emission spectra. Data for other samples is assigned to the supplementary information (Figure S1).

In the next step, we wanted to observe if the shift in emission peak Δλem with respect to the lowest and highest doping density, Δλem =
|
λ
e m
6
−
λ
e m
1
|
,
had any correlation with λex and this is depicted in Fig. S4, here 1 and 6 in superscripts referred to the lowest doping(S1) and highest doping(S6) samples. The data was fitted to a straight line with a negative slope of 0.084 and chi-squared value of 0.98. At lower excitation the difference was large while at higher λex this difference remained within experimental error.

Optical bandgap of these samples were determined from the well-known Tauc plot (See Figure S3 (a)) (Supplementary Information). This signified that the samples were becoming more conducting with doping. The un-doped samples were semi-metal in nature while the optical bandgap of highest doped sample was ≈5.3 eV. The bandgap versus N/C plot yielded a linear plot which could be defined by
Y
B G
=
a
B G
+
b
B G
(
N C
)
. Here YBG is the bandgap and bBG is the coefficient of decrease of bandgap with the N/C ratio. When the data in Fig. 4(b) was least-squares fitted to linear dependence according to eqn. (3), it yielded a negative slope of 9.3 (chi-squared = 0.97).

In the Tauc plot the quantity (αhν)1/2 is on the ordinate, where α is the absorption coefficient of the material intersects the quantity hν (the energy of the light) on the abscissa. The resulting plot gives rise to a distinct linear region (onset of absorption). Extrapolating this linear region to the absciassa, we obtained the energy of the optical bandgap of samples. In our case at the absorption cut-off wavelength (360 nm), value of (αhν)1/2 tends to zero, so the linear region was no longer available to determine the bandgap. Though the bandgap was about 3.5 eV, according to the formula of Eg (eV) = 1240/λg (nm), but we could not measure this value using Tauc plot analysis.

The Quantum Yield(QY) of N-GQDs was determined relative to a reference compound of known QY. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as the reference, which has a fixed and known
Q
Y
r e f
value of 54% [34].The QY of the samples were calculated by comparing the integrated FL intensity
(
λ
e x
≈ 350 nm) and the absorbance values at the excitation wavelength. We observed quantum yield for N-GQDs in the range of 0.70 ± 0.05, consistent with the reported data [35].

Films of N-GQD prepared on silicon (SiO2) substrate showed interesting conduction properties both under forward and reverse bias conditions (Fig. 4). The non-linearity in IV characteristics confirmed that the V/I ratio of these samples are not constant (non-ohmic behavior). These samples distinctively revealed semiconductor-like behavior. Depending on the doping under the forward, and reverse biased condition the current varied in a variety of ranges with applied voltage. Much like the diode characteristics, these samples continued to block current flowing through it until the reverse voltage became larger than its breakdown voltage VBDresulting in a sudden increase in reverse current producing a fairly linear downward curve. This reverse breakdown voltage VBDis controlled by the doping density of the samples. This effect is very similar to Zener break-down. For the lightly doped samples the breakdown is dominated by the avalanche effect rather than that of Zener effect. Consequently, the breakdown voltage is higher (over 5 V) for the lowest doping and it decreased to 3 V with the degree of doping making it much superior for different applications. A drastic change in the knee-voltage was observed with doping density in the forward bias region. Knee-voltage decreased with increase in the doping density. In the case of highest doping, knee voltage was just 0.35 V, which was typical forward voltage drop of Germanium diode (0.3 V)making it a promising candidate in electronics. All the diode parameters were found to be dependent on the doping density. TheI-V-profiles were examined multiple times to ascertain the reproducibility of data. Table 1 summarizes the observed diode parameters.

IV characteristics determined at different temperatures show that at room temperature the samples depict good diode behavior with high gain. As temperature was reduced a drastic change in reverse bias characteristics was observed. Avalanching was observed at a higher reverse voltage. As the temperature was lowered, an increase in leakage current density was observed and at 170 K leakage current was the highest (2.5 mA). The diode leakage current or ‘dark saturation current’ did undergo an increase at lower temperature regime.

At low T, the sample revealed a sharp breakdown voltage and a dramatic reduction in the dynamic resistance (slope of VI curve) was observed. The avalanching was highly enhanced by temperature. The IV curve shifted approximately by 20 mV/ ° K for a given current (see Fig. 3). These showed typical n-type semiconductor behavior, and the mobility was similar to the N-doped graphene quantum dots annealed in vacuum [4].The reason is that nitrogen atoms incorporate mainly into the edge of GQDs, and few charged impurities are introduced, which do not degrade the mobility of GQDs.

Interestingly, the knee and breakdown voltage changed linearly (Fig. 4(a)) with the N/C ratio which could be given by
(1)
Y
B V / K V
=
a
B V / K V
+
b
B V / K V
(
N C
)

The breakdown and knee voltages are designated as BV and KV in the suffix. Remarkably, the slope of both the plots were found to be same, b
BV = b
KV = 41, and the chi-squared value was >0.96. The optical and electronic properties of the undoped GQDs have been compared with the data reported in the ref. [42]. The calculated (002) interlayer spacing is 0.4 nm (HR-TEM data) which matches well with the reported data [43].

In summary, we demonstrated an efficient and convenient route to synthesize highly crystalline and ultrahigh fluorescent N-doped graphene quantum dots with high nitrogen content prepared by microwave-heating of glucose in NH3solution at room temperature. The nitrogen concentration doped in the graphene was 6.4–10.60% (w/v). This low-cost, one-step approach is eco-friendly and suitable for large-scale production.

A material comparison of different graphene based systems reveals that graphite oxide with an optical bandgap of 5–6 eV is an insulator with diamagnetic property [36] while graphene oxide through diamagnetic behave like a semiconductor with a bandgap of 3–5 eV [37–39], In comparison graphene is a zero bandgap semi-metals that shows diamagnetic ferromagnetic attributes [40,41].

Nitrogen doping has been shown as an effective way to tailor the properties of graphene and render its potential for use in various applications related to electronics. Interestingly, graphene quantum dots are semiconductors with a bandgap of 3–4 eV. Our samples had bandgaps in the window 5.9 to 5.3 eV decreasing with nitrogen doping concentration. These samples have breakdown voltage in the range of 3–5.3 V and can be used depending on the applications requirement. The reverse bias voltage leading to Zener breakdown is tunable during the fabrication of devices using these samples. These samples have good efficiency for a wide temperature range confirming less thermal fluctuation and noise. Electrical transport properties of these samples are highly enriched by nitrogen doping making these samples good candidates in the semiconductor world. This study has shown that N-graphene has a better electron transfer efficiency than pristine graphene, rendering its potential use for a range of semiconductor applications and electrochemical sensing.
The authors declare no competing conflict of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by UGC-DRS-SAP funding. KR acknowledges receipt of DST-Inspire Faculty award from Department of Science and Technology, Government of India. We are thankful to the Advanced Research Instrumentation Facility (AIRF) of the University for allowing us access to the TEM, SEM/EDX, FTIR and Raman facility.
